entries.intro: I saw something moving through the trees near {subject_focus_the} two nights ago. Tall, thin, walking upright but not like any person I know.
details: It moved between the trunks without making a sound. Not even the leaves rustled when it passed.
The shape was wrong: arms too long, head too narrow. Like someone stretched a person out and forgot to stop.
It paused once and turned toward me. I couldn't see a face, but I could feel it looking.
reactions: I've lived near those woods my whole life. I know every animal that walks through them. That wasn't one.
I told my neighbor and she just went quiet. Then she said her boy saw the same thing last week.

statCheck.pass: When it turned, I caught a glint: something reflective where its eyes should be, like polished metal catching moonlight.
statCheck.fail: In the dark, everything looks like it could be something. I have scanned that tree line a dozen times since and nothing stands out.

entries.intro: A group of us spotted what looked like a massive elk near {subject_focus_the}, but it had too many antler points. Way too many.
details: We counted at least thirty points before it moved behind the ridge. A normal bull might have twelve.
Its antlers had a faint shimmer to them, like frost, but it was the middle of a warm afternoon.
reactions: The hunters are excited. I think it's bad luck, personally. Hard to say for certain.
It didn't run when it saw us. Just stood there, chewing, like we weren't worth worrying about.

entries.intro: There's been a creature drinking from the stream near {subject_focus_the} at dawn. Nobody can agree on what it is.
details: The farmer says it's a wolf. The trapper says it's too big for a wolf. The tanner says it's got scales.
It comes at the same time every morning, drinks for exactly as long as it takes to count to twenty, then leaves.
It always approaches from the east and leaves heading west. Never varies the route.
reactions: I set up early one morning to see it myself. Overslept. Typical.
Whatever it is, it hasn't bothered anyone yet. But I'd like to know what I'm sharing a water source with.

statCheck.pass: I found tracks at the stream bank. They're canine, but with a fifth toe set higher up, like a thumb. I've never seen that on any wolf.
statCheck.fail: So many animals drink there that the mud is a mess of overlapping prints. Cannot isolate anything unusual.

entries.intro: Something flew over {subject_focus_the} last night. Big enough to block out the stars as it passed.
details: No wingbeats. It just glided, silent, from the north ridge to the southern tree line in about ten seconds.
The shadow it cast was wider than a barn. My lantern flickered when it went overhead.
reactions: I keep telling myself it was a cloud. Clouds don't move that fast in a straight line, though.
The goats were huddled in the corner of the pen this morning. They don't huddle unless something scared them.

entries.intro: A child near {subject_focus_the} says she's been playing with a fox that talks. We all laughed until she started repeating things no child her age should know.
details: She described the layout of the abandoned mine in detail. She's never been there. None of us have taken her.
She says the fox has silver fur and only comes when she's alone. It taught her a song in a language nobody here recognizes.
The song, when she hums it, makes the dogs whine and leave the room.
reactions: Her mother is terrified. Her father thinks she has a good imagination. I think the father is wrong.
I went to the spot where she says they play. Found small paw prints, but they were oddly deep for something fox-sized.

statCheck.pass: I had the elder listen to the child's song. He went pale and said it was an old warding chant: one that hasn't been spoken in this region for generations.
statCheck.fail: It sounds like any other made-up tune, honestly. Without context, there is no telling if it means anything.
